text: Dental caries appears on radiographs as a radiolucent (dark) area where demineralisation has reduced the mineral density of enamel or dentin. Early enamel caries is confined to the outer enamel and does not cross the dentino-enamel junction.

Proximal (interproximal) caries typically presents as a triangular or notched radiolucency below the contact point of adjacent teeth, with the apex of the triangle pointing toward the dentino-enamel junction.

The ICDAS/ICCMS classification stages caries radiographically by depth: confined to enamel (E1, E2), reaching the outer or inner third of dentin (D1), or extending into the inner dentin near the pulp (D2, D3).
Radiographic detection of caries is most reliable for proximal surfaces; occlusal and early lesions are frequently underestimated on radiographs and require clinical correlation.
A caries lesion is a localised process of the affected tooth; radiographic interpretation should be confined to the demineralised area of that tooth and not attributed to adjacent structures.

Management of an early-to-moderate caries lesion ranges from non-operative remineralisation (fluoride, sealants) for enamel lesions to operative restoration once the lesion is cavitated or reaches dentin.
Deep caries is a carious lesion that radiographically extends into the inner third of dentin and approaches the pulp chamber, with little intact dentin remaining between the lesion and the pulp.
Proximity of a deep caries lesion to the pulp raises the risk of pulpal inflammation (pulpitis); the radiographic distance between the radiolucency and the pulp horn is a key interpretive feature.

Deep caries assessment combines the lesion's depth (pulp proximity) with the surrounding pulpal and periapical context, because the treatment decision depends on whether the pulp is likely vital, reversibly inflamed, or necrotic.
Treatment options for deep caries include selective (stepwise) caries removal to preserve pulp vitality, indirect pulp capping, or root canal therapy if irreversible pulpitis or necrosis is present.
A deep caries lesion that has produced a periapical radiolucency indicates likely pulp necrosis and endodontic involvement rather than a simple restorable lesion.
A periapical lesion appears as a radiolucency at or around the apex (tip) of a tooth root, reflecting bone resorption secondary to pulpal disease.
Periapical radiolucencies are most commonly the sequel of pulp necrosis; the offending tooth typically tests non-vital, and the lesion is continuous with the periodontal ligament space at the apex.

The periapical radiolucency of apical periodontitis is highly localised to the root apex; loss of the apical lamina dura and a well- or ill-defined radiolucency centred on the apex are characteristic.

The Periapical Index (PAI) grades apical periodontitis from 1 (healthy periapical bone) to 5 (severe periodontitis with exacerbating features) based on the radiographic appearance of the periapical bone.

Differential diagnosis of a periapical radiolucency includes periapical granuloma, radicular cyst, and periapical abscess; definitive distinction often requires histopathology, but all imply endodontic origin when associated with a non-vital tooth.

Management of a periapical lesion of endodontic origin is root canal treatment or, where this fails or is not feasible, apical surgery or extraction; healing is monitored by reduction of the periapical radiolucency over time.
An impacted tooth is one that has failed to erupt into the dental arch within its expected developmental window, most commonly the mandibular third molar (wisdom tooth).
Winter's classification describes the angulation of an impacted mandibular third molar relative to the second molar: mesioangular, distoangular, vertical, or horizontal.

Radiographic assessment of an impacted tooth requires the surrounding context: the relationship of the crown and roots to the adjacent second molar, the ramus, and the inferior alveolar (mandibular) canal.

Signs of a close relationship between an impacted lower third molar and the mandibular canal include darkening of the root, interruption of the canal's white lines, and diversion of the canal, indicating elevated nerve-injury risk on extraction.

Because impaction is defined by a tooth's position relative to neighbouring anatomical structures, its radiographic interpretation legitimately involves features beyond the tooth crown itself, such as the adjacent tooth and bone.

Management of an impacted tooth ranges from observation of an asymptomatic, disease-free impaction to surgical removal when associated with pericoronitis, caries, cyst formation, or damage to the adjacent tooth.

A panoramic radiograph (OPG) is a two-dimensional projection; overlapping anatomy and geometric distortion mean radiographic findings should be confirmed with clinical examination and, where indicated, additional imaging.

FDI two-digit tooth notation identifies a tooth by quadrant (1 upper right, 2 upper left, 3 lower left, 4 lower right) followed by tooth position from the midline (1 central incisor to 8 third molar); e.g., 36 is the lower-left first molar.

A radiolucency is a dark area on a radiograph indicating reduced mineral density (e.g., caries, bone resorption); a radiopacity is a light area indicating increased density (e.g., enamel, restorations, bone).
An AI-generated radiographic finding is a decision-support suggestion and is not a definitive diagnosis; the responsible clinician must verify every finding before acting on it.
